ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product class; no specific curry preparation class exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Aubergines (eggplants) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for eggplant-based dish; no composite curry code available in FoodEx2 for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for eggplant curry as a composite dish. A00LC (aubergines/eggplants) used as primary ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for eggplant curry as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of eggplant curry as a side dish at a Singaporean mamak stall is approximately 150 g, representing one ladle portion served alongside rice or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Eggplant Curry — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14080011 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Eggplant — University of Sydney GI Database; eggplant (aubergine) GI approximately 15 (low). Curry preparation does not substantially alter GI given low carbohydrate content.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for eggplant curry as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is based on the GI of eggplant (aubergine) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient, consistent with published values for eggplant in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.95) / 100 ≈ 1.2, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 5.3 × 1.5 = 7.95 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 437 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size of eggplant curry to no more than 100 g per meal and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content of 437 mg/100 g exceeds recommended thresholds; using low-sodium curry powder and increasing the proportion of eggplant relative to sauce can help reduce sodium density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.3 g/100 g), low GI (~15), and moderate dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Eggplant curry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content of 5.3 g/100 g, low glycaemic index of approximately 15, and 2.3 g/100 g dietary fibre which slows glucose absorption; however, the fat content of 8.4 g/100 g contributes to caloric density and should be monitored in the context of overall energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (437 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (8.4 g/100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors, partially offset by dietary fibre and plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, eggplant curry presents a moderate concern due to its sodium content of 437 mg/100 g and total fat of 8.4 g/100 g; the dish's plant-based composition, dietary fibre of 2.3 g/100 g, and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) provide partial cardiovascular benefit, but sodium reduction in preparation is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, chili) contain solanine which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who have confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid eggplant curry, as eggplant, tomato, and chili are all nightshade vegetables containing solanine; those without confirmed nightshade sensitivity may benefit from the anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerols present in the turmeric and ginger components of the curry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eggplant curry (brinjal curry) is a popular vegetable-based dish in Singaporean mamak cuisine, consisting of tender eggplant (aubergine/brinjal) slow-cooked in a spiced curry sauce made from onion, tomato, chili, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, and cooking oil. The dish has a soft, yielding texture with the eggplant absorbing the aromatic curry sauce, producing a rich, mildly spicy, and savoury flavour profile. It is fully plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and halal, making it widely accessible across Singapore's diverse population. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 104 kcal, 1.8 g protein, 8.4 g fat, 5.3 g carbohydrate, 2.3 g dietary fibre, and 437 mg sodium. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~15) make it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, while the elevated sodium content of 437 mg/100 g raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice, roti canai, or chapati at mamak stalls and Indian-Muslim restaurants throughout Singapore and Malaysia. Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols) contribute functional food properties. Nightshade-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the solanine content of eggplant, tomato, and chili.
common_user_queries: Is brinjal curry suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080011, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate based on eggplant GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component to medium; however, the primary nutrient data confidence remains high.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source for this record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this mamak eggplant curry preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F14080011 as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g (1.2 g) and sugar_g (2.8 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for eggplant curry (closest analogous composite) as these fields are not individually reported in the HPB record. Cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero is consistent with plant-based food composition. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on eggplant (aubergine) GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite dish GI entry exists. Input record glycemic_index was 0 with confidence 0.321 from an unspecified embedded GI database — this has been replaced with the University of Sydney analogous estimate. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 (eggplant food product) and FoodEx2 A00LC (aubergines/eggplants) used as closest_parent matches; no composite curry-specific codes exist in either system.
